title: The Gap Between Letting Go
description: Milo has Chapter One (letting go) and Chapter Two (letting go faster). Chapter Three has been blank for a year. He wants the party to fill it in — not through writing, but through doing.

offer.speaker: Scholar Milo
offer.text: You read the guide. Or carried it, which is the first step toward reading. I have a confession: Chapter Three doesn't exist. I've been trying to write it for a year.

offer.speaker: Scholar Milo
offer.text: It's about the gap. Between letting go and having it taken from you. I know it's there. I can't articulate it. Words are a terrible technology for gaps.

offer.speaker: Scholar Milo
offer.text: So you'll write it for me. Not with a quill. With doing. Three exercises. Report back after each.

accept.speaker: Scholar Milo
accept.text: First: let the system fight twice in a row. No hands. Second: fight once with nothing but your hands — no abilities, no cleverness. Third: do something that should fail. And don't fail.

accept.speaker: Scholar Milo
accept.text: In that order. The order is the argument.

decline.speaker: Scholar Milo
decline.text: Chapter Three waits. It's very patient. It's been blank for a year — blankness is mostly patience.

in_progress.speaker: Scholar Milo
in_progress.text: The exercises aren't tests. Tests have right answers. These have true ones. Different thing.

ready_to_turn_in.speaker: Scholar Milo
ready_to_turn_in.text: You did all three. I can see it in how you're standing. People stand differently on both sides of the gap at once.

complete.speaker: Scholar Milo
complete.text: Chapter Three: 'The gap between letting go and having it taken from you is exactly as wide as you decide it is. Letting go is a choice. Having it taken is also a choice, made by the part of you that stopped choosing.'

complete.speaker: Scholar Milo
complete.text: 'The gap is where you live. The gap is where everything interesting happens. I'm not sure this is a chapter. It might be a life.'

complete.speaker: Scholar Milo
complete.text: I'm going to publish it anyway. Here — the working pages. Keep them. They function better as an object than as a manuscript, which is true of most philosophy.

objectives.description: Complete two consecutive encounters on full autobattle, no manual intervention.
Tell Milo how letting go went.
Win a manual battle using only basic attacks — no abilities.
Tell Milo how holding on went.
Do something in battle that should fail — and don't fail.

cross_world_connection: W4: the foreman's hidden watercolors include an abstract piece that is a visual rendering of 'the gap' — a space between two solid forms, lit from the inside. Extra line if chapter_three_pages is in inventory.
_wiring_notes: The three custom flags are BATTLE-TELEMETRY emissions, owned by cowir-battle/cowir-main, wire when convenient: (1) quest_world1_chapter_three_autobattle_run = two consecutive battle victories with zero manual actions taken (autobattle active for all PCs, no player input mid-battle); (2) quest_world1_chapter_three_basics_only = battle victory where every player action was a basic attack (no abilities, no items, no defer/advance is fine to allow); (3) quest_world1_chapter_three_impossible = interpretively wide by design — acceptable emitters: victory while under-leveled vs a danger-zone enemy, victory with a lucky crit from <10% HP, a one-HP victory (event_flag_one_hp_victory can double-emit), or an exploit-flagged win. Milo's reaction dialogue can branch on which emitter fired (later polish). The quest is authored data-first: it sits inert in the log until the flags get wired — QuestSystem simply won't progress steps 2/4/6. Sequential order is a v1 simplification of the doc's any-order design; the order IS thematically motivated (let go -> hold on -> the gap), noted in accept dialogue.
